problem text: Three individuals, labeled A, B, and C, have distinct probabilities of arriving late: A and B are each late with probability 1/2, and C is late with probability 1/3. Assuming the lateness of each person is independent, what is the likelihood that exactly two individuals arrive late?
problem solution: To determine the probability that exactly two individuals are late, we enumerate the events in which exactly two of A, B, and C are late. First, note that $$P(A\text{ late})=\frac12,\ P(B\text{ late})=\frac12,\ P(C\text{ late})=\frac13,$$ and these events are mutually independent. We compute $$P(\text{A,B late, C not late})=\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac23\right)=\frac16,$$ $$P(\text{A,C late, B not late})=\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac13\right)=\frac1{12},$$ and $$P(\text{B,C late, A not late})=\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac12\right)\left(\frac13\right)=\frac1{12}.$$ Adding these results yields $$\frac16 + \frac1{12} + \frac1{12} = \frac13.$$ Hence, the probability that exactly two individuals are late is $$\frac13.$$
problem hint: Consider all ways to pick exactly two of the three individuals to be late.
